subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Open your eyes at dawn and the world appears. The pink glow over the savanna after the Gu rains, the silver flash of sunlight on the Indian Ocean, the deep green of a mango leaf, the golden lamp of the crescent moon that marks the start of a new lunar month, the letters on this very page, all of it reaches you because of one thing: light. Light is a form of energy that your eyes can detect. Without it, there is no seeing. In this unit you will learn how light travels, how it bounces off mirrors and the sea, how it bends as it passes into a glass of water, how lenses and your own eye focus it, and how white sunlight is secretly a mixture of every colour of the rainbow.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Science 0893 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines and describe evidence for this, such as shadows and beams.
Describe the difference between luminous and non-luminous objects and explain how we see non-luminous objects by reflected light.
State and use the law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
Measure angles of incidence and reflection from the normal and solve simple numerical problems.
Explain refraction as the bending of light when it changes speed passing from one material into another.
Describe how convex and concave lenses bend light, and explain how a converging lens forms an image.
Label the main parts of the human eye and explain how the eye focuses light onto the retina.
Explain dispersion and how a prism or rain droplets split white light into a spectrum.
Describe the difference between mixing coloured light and mixing paints, and explain why objects appear the colours they do.

concepts.title: Light — part 1
concepts.explanation: Open your eyes at dawn and the world appears. The pink glow over the savanna after the Gu rains, the silver flash of sunlight on the Indian Ocean, the deep green of a mango leaf, the golden lamp of the crescent moon that marks the start of a new lunar month, the letters on this very page, all of it reaches you because of one thing: light. Light is a form of energy that your eyes can detect. Without it, there is no seeing. In this unit you will learn how light travels, how it bounces off mirrors and the sea, how it…
concepts.example: You already know a great deal about light without realising it. You know that you must not stare at the sun, that a mirror shows your face, that a straw in a glass of water looks bent, that the road ahead can shimmer…

concepts.title: Light — part 2
concepts.explanation: You already know a great deal about light without realising it. You know that you must not stare at the sun, that a mirror shows your face, that a straw in a glass of water looks bent, that the road ahead can shimmer like water on a hot afternoon. This unit gives you the science behind all of these everyday wonders. Understanding light also has real, practical value: it is how spectacles help Grandmother read again, how a camera captures a photograph, how solar panels turn sunlight into electricity, and how you…
concepts.example: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Ask: "Why can I see the moon at night even though the moon does not make its own light?" Then ask your tutor to explain the difference between a luminous object and a non-luminous object using…

concepts.title: Light — part 3
concepts.explanation: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Ask: "Why can I see the moon at night even though the moon does not make its own light?" Then ask your tutor to explain the difference between a luminous object and a non-luminous object using examples from where I live.
concepts.example: Explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines and describe evidence for this, such as shadows and beams.

concepts.title: Light — part 4
concepts.explanation: Explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines and describe evidence for this, such as shadows and beams.
concepts.example: Describe the difference between luminous and non-luminous objects and explain how we see non-luminous objects by reflected light.

concepts.title: Light — part 5

explorations.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
explorations.context: To find evidence that light travels in straight lines.
explorations.prompt: Write down what you predict: if light travels in a straight line, what will happen when you try to shine a torch through three cards whose holes are not lined up?
explorations.answer: When did the light reach your eye, and when did it not? If light could bend around corners, what would you have seen when the holes were not lined up? What does this experiment prove about how light travels?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find evidence that light travels in straight lines.

explorations.title: The Law of Reflection with a Mirror
explorations.context: To test whether the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
explorations.prompt: Predict: when you shine a narrow beam at a mirror, how will the angle it leaves compare with the angle it arrived at?
explorations.answer: How did the angle of reflection compare with the angle of incidence each time? Do your results support the law of reflection? Explain any small differences. Why was it important to measure from the normal and not from the mirror?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To test whether the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

explorations.title: The Bent Straw (Refraction in Water)
explorations.context: To observe refraction and see how water bends light.
explorations.prompt: Predict what a straight straw will look like when half of it is under water and you look from the side.
explorations.answer: Did the straw really bend? What actually bent? Why did the hidden coin seem to appear as the water rose? Which way does light bend as it leaves the water and enters the air?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe refraction and see how water bends light.

explorations.title: Splitting Light into a Rainbow
explorations.context: To split white light into a spectrum and prove white light is a mixture of colours.
explorations.prompt: Predict how many colours you will see and in what order.
explorations.answer: What does this show about what white sunlight is made of? Why do the colours come out separated instead of mixed? How is this experiment similar to a rainbow after the Gu rains?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To split white light into a spectrum and prove white light is a mixture of colours.

explorations.title: Why Objects Have Colour
explorations.context: To investigate how the colour of the light changes the colour we see.
explorations.prompt: Predict what a blue cloth will look like under red-coloured light, and why.
explorations.answer: What happened to the blue cloth under red light? Explain why. Why did the white cloth take on the colour of whatever light you shone? Why did the black cloth stay dark under every colour of light?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate how the colour of the light changes the colour we see.

methods.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
methods.example: To find evidence that light travels in straight lines.
steps: Make a small hole in the centre of each card at exactly the same height, using the pencil.
Stand the three cards up in a row, evenly spaced, with all three holes lined up straight.
In a darkened room, shine the torch through the first hole and look from the far side. Record whether the light reaches your eye.
Now slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer lined up with the others.
Shine the torch again and record whether the light still reaches your eye.

methods.title: The Law of Reflection with a Mirror
methods.example: To test whether the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
steps: Lay the paper flat and stand the mirror upright on it. Draw a line along the base of the mirror.
Draw the normal: a dotted line at 90 degrees to the mirror line, at the point the beam will hit.
In a dark room, shine the thin beam at that point at an angle. Mark two dots along the incoming beam and two along the reflected beam with your pencil.
Remove the mirror and torch. Use the ruler to join the dots into rays that meet at the point on the mirror line.
Measure the angle of incidence and the angle of reflection from the normal. Record both.

methods.title: The Bent Straw (Refraction in Water)
methods.example: To observe refraction and see how water bends light.
steps: Fill the glass about three-quarters full with clean water.
Stand the straw in the glass and look at it from the side, roughly at the level of the water.
Draw exactly what you see. Note where the straw appears to bend.
Now place a coin at the bottom of a bowl. Move back until the rim just hides the coin from view.
Keeping still, ask someone to slowly pour water into the bowl. Watch the coin.

methods.title: Splitting Light into a Rainbow
methods.example: To split white light into a spectrum and prove white light is a mixture of colours.
steps: Place the tray of water where sunlight from a window reaches it.
Slide the mirror into the water, leaning it against one side at an angle.
Adjust the mirror so sunlight strikes the underwater part and reflects up onto the white paper or wall.
The wedge of water in front of the mirror acts as a prism. Look for a band of colours on the paper.
Record the colours you see and their order.

methods.title: Why Objects Have Colour
methods.example: To investigate how the colour of the light changes the colour we see.
steps: Cover the torch with a red wrapper to make a red beam.
In a dark room, shine the red light on each coloured cloth in turn and record its apparent colour.
Repeat with a blue wrapper, then a green wrapper.
Note especially what happens to a cloth whose colour is NOT in the light you are shining.
Observation Sheet

workedExamples.title: using the law of reflection
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through a problem step by step, the way you should in your notebook. Hassan shines a torch at a flat mirror. The incident ray hits the mirror so that it makes an angle of 50 degrees with the mirror surface. What is the angle of reflection,…
workedExamples.solution: Find the angle of incidence first. The angle is given from the mirror surface, but we must measure from the normal. The normal is at 90 degrees to the mirror. So the angle of incidence = 90 - 50 = 40 degrees. Apply the law of reflection. The law says angle of incidence = angle of reflection, so i = r. State the answer. Since i = 40 degrees, the angle of reflection r = 40 degrees (measured from the normal). Check it makes sense. The reflected ray also makes 90 - 40 = 50 degrees with the mirror surface, mirroring…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curves (b) straight lines (c) circles (d) zig-zags on its own
workedExamples.solution: (b) straight lines. This is shown by sharp shadows and narrow beams.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The imaginary line at 90 degrees to a mirror is called the: (a) ray (b) normal (c) incident line (d) spectrum
workedExamples.solution: (b) normal. The normal is drawn at 90 degrees to the surface and is the reference for all angles.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The law of reflection says the angle of incidence equals the angle of: (a) refraction (b) reflection (c) the mirror (d) dispersion
workedExamples.solution: (b) reflection. The law states angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A ray hits a mirror at 30 degrees to the normal. The angle of reflection is: (a) 60 degrees (b) 30 degrees (c) 90 degrees (d) 15 degrees
workedExamples.solution: (b) 30 degrees. By the law of reflection the reflected angle equals the incident angle.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: The bending of light as it passes from air into water is called: (a) reflection (b) refraction (c) dispersion (d) scattering
workedExamples.solution: (b) refraction. Refraction is the bending of light when it changes speed between materials.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A convex lens is: (a) thinner in the middle (b) thicker in the middle (c) flat (d) always black
workedExamples.solution: (b) thicker in the middle. A convex (converging) lens is thicker at its centre.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The part of the eye where the image forms is the: (a) pupil (b) iris (c) retina (d) cornea
workedExamples.solution: (c) retina. The image forms on the light-sensitive retina at the back of the eye.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a luminous object? (a) the moon (b) a mirror (c) the sun (d) a mango
practice.answer: (c) the sun. A luminous object makes its own light; the moon, a mirror and a mango only reflect light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curves (b) straight lines (c) circles (d) zig-zags on its own
practice.answer: (b) straight lines. This is shown by sharp shadows and narrow beams.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The imaginary line at 90 degrees to a mirror is called the: (a) ray (b) normal (c) incident line (d) spectrum
practice.answer: (b) normal. The normal is drawn at 90 degrees to the surface and is the reference for all angles.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The law of reflection says the angle of incidence equals the angle of: (a) refraction (b) reflection (c) the mirror (d) dispersion
practice.answer: (b) reflection. The law states angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A ray hits a mirror at 30 degrees to the normal. The angle of reflection is: (a) 60 degrees (b) 30 degrees (c) 90 degrees (d) 15 degrees
practice.answer: (b) 30 degrees. By the law of reflection the reflected angle equals the incident angle.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The bending of light as it passes from air into water is called: (a) reflection (b) refraction (c) dispersion (d) scattering
practice.answer: (b) refraction. Refraction is the bending of light when it changes speed between materials.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A convex lens is: (a) thinner in the middle (b) thicker in the middle (c) flat (d) always black
practice.answer: (b) thicker in the middle. A convex (converging) lens is thicker at its centre.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The part of the eye where the image forms is the: (a) pupil (b) iris (c) retina (d) cornea
practice.answer: (c) retina. The image forms on the light-sensitive retina at the back of the eye.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Splitting white light into colours with a prism is called: (a) reflection (b) refraction (c) dispersion (d) absorption
practice.answer: (c) dispersion. Dispersion splits white light into its spectrum of colours.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The three primary colours of LIGHT are: (a) red, yellow, blue (b) red, green, blue (c) red, white, black (d) green, yellow, orange
practice.answer: (b) red, green, blue. These are the primary colours of light; they add to make white.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A red cloth looks red because it: (a) makes red light (b) absorbs red and reflects the rest (c) reflects red and absorbs the rest (d) lets all light through
practice.answer: (c) reflects red and absorbs the rest. The cloth reflects only red light to your eyes.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A blue cloth under pure red light looks: (a) blue (b) red (c) white (d) black
practice.answer: (d) black. There is no blue light in a red beam for the blue cloth to reflect, so it looks black.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
activities.materials: Three pieces of card or stiff paper (a cereal box works well); A torch or a phone torch; A pencil, a ruler, and some sticky tack or small lumps of dough to stand the cards up
steps: Make a small hole in the centre of each card at exactly the same height, using the pencil.
Stand the three cards up in a row, evenly spaced, with all three holes lined up straight.
In a darkened room, shine the torch through the first hole and look from the far side. Record whether the light reaches your eye.
Now slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer lined up with the others.
Shine the torch again and record whether the light still reaches your eye.

activities.title: The Law of Reflection with a Mirror
activities.materials: A small flat mirror stood upright against a book; A torch (cover the front with paper that has a thin slit cut in it to make a narrow beam); A sheet of paper, a ruler, a pencil, and a protractor if you have one
steps: Lay the paper flat and stand the mirror upright on it. Draw a line along the base of the mirror.
Draw the normal: a dotted line at 90 degrees to the mirror line, at the point the beam will hit.
In a dark room, shine the thin beam at that point at an angle. Mark two dots along the incoming beam and two along the reflected beam with your pencil.
Remove the mirror and torch. Use the ruler to join the dots into rays that meet at the point on the mirror line.
Measure the angle of incidence and the angle of reflection from the normal. Record both.

activities.title: The Bent Straw (Refraction in Water)
activities.materials: A clear glass or jar; Clean water; A straight straw, a pencil, or a spoon; A coin and a bowl (for the second part)
steps: Fill the glass about three-quarters full with clean water.
Stand the straw in the glass and look at it from the side, roughly at the level of the water.
Draw exactly what you see. Note where the straw appears to bend.
Now place a coin at the bottom of a bowl. Move back until the rim just hides the coin from view.
Keeping still, ask someone to slowly pour water into the bowl. Watch the coin.

activities.title: Splitting Light into a Rainbow
activities.materials: A shallow tray or bowl of water; A small flat mirror; A sheet of white paper or a white wall; Bright sunlight from a window (or a glass prism if you have one)
steps: Place the tray of water where sunlight from a window reaches it.
Slide the mirror into the water, leaning it against one side at an angle.
Adjust the mirror so sunlight strikes the underwater part and reflects up onto the white paper or wall.
The wedge of water in front of the mirror acts as a prism. Look for a band of colours on the paper.
Record the colours you see and their order.

activities.title: Why Objects Have Colour
activities.materials: A torch; Coloured see-through sweet wrappers or thin coloured plastic (red, blue, green); Small squares of coloured cloth or coloured paper (red, blue, green, white, black); A dark room
steps: Cover the torch with a red wrapper to make a red beam.
In a dark room, shine the red light on each coloured cloth in turn and record its apparent colour.
Repeat with a blue wrapper, then a green wrapper.
Note especially what happens to a cloth whose colour is NOT in the light you are shining.
Observation Sheet

activities.title: Observe: Light — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of light — part 6 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Makes its own light (sun, fire, torch).
Seen only by reflected light (moon, goat, page).
A straight line showing the direction light travels.
Imaginary line at 90 degrees to a surface; used to measure angles.
Angle between the incoming ray and the normal.
Angle between the reflected ray and the normal.
Bending of light as it changes speed between materials.
Thicker in the middle; converges (brings together) light.
Thinner in the middle; diverges (spreads out) light.
Splitting of white light into a spectrum of colours.
The band of colours in white light.

vocabulary.meaning: Makes its own light (sun, fire, torch).
vocabulary.example: Describe the difference between luminous and non-luminous objects and explain how we see non-luminous objects by reflected light.

vocabulary.meaning: Seen only by reflected light (moon, goat, page).
vocabulary.example: Describe the difference between luminous and non-luminous objects and explain how we see non-luminous objects by reflected light.

vocabulary.meaning: A straight line showing the direction light travels.
vocabulary.example: An imaginary line drawn at 90 degrees (perpendicular) to a surface at the point where a ray hits.
Measure angles of incidence and reflection from the normal and solve simple numerical problems.

vocabulary.meaning: Imaginary line at 90 degrees to a surface; used to measure angles.
vocabulary.example: Measure angles of incidence and reflection from the normal and solve simple numerical problems.

vocabulary.meaning: Angle between the incoming ray and the normal.
vocabulary.example: The angle of incidence (often written as the letter i) is between the incident ray and the normal.

vocabulary.meaning: Angle between the reflected ray and the normal.
vocabulary.example: The angle of reflection (written as r) is between the reflected ray and the normal.

vocabulary.meaning: Bending of light as it changes speed between materials.
vocabulary.example: Explain refraction as the bending of light when it changes speed passing from one material into another.

vocabulary.meaning: Thicker in the middle; converges (brings together) light.
vocabulary.example: A convex lens is thicker in the middle than at the edges.

vocabulary.meaning: Thinner in the middle; diverges (spreads out) light.
vocabulary.example: A concave lens is thinner in the middle than at the edges.

vocabulary.meaning: Splitting of white light into a spectrum of colours.
vocabulary.example: Explain dispersion and how a prism or rain droplets split white light into a spectrum.

vocabulary.meaning: The band of colours in white light.
vocabulary.example: Explain dispersion and how a prism or rain droplets split white light into a spectrum.

commonMistakes: The mirror is not the reference line.
The moon makes no light of its own.
Thinking light always bends in water.
Light on the normal does not bend.
Mixing paint rules with light rules.
Paints subtract colour; lights add colour.
Saying a red object gives out red light.
It makes no light of its own.

connections.text: light is one of the forms of energy, alongside heat, sound and electricity.
Sound (next unit): like light, sound is energy that travels, but it needs a material and moves far more slowly, which is why you see lightning before you hear thunder.
understanding the eye links to how the body senses the world.
rainbows, blue skies and red sunsets connect light to the atmosphere and the seasons.
cameras, spectacles, mirrors and solar panels all use the science of light.
Watch the sky at sunset over the ocean and explain the red colours using scattering.

realProblems.prompt: Amina sees a shimmering 'pool of water' on the hot road to Garissa that disappears as she nears it. Explain what she is really seeing and why.
realProblems.answer: She is seeing a mirage. Hot, thin air just above the tarmac bends (refracts) light from the sky upward into her eyes, so an image of the bright sky appears on the road and looks like water. There is no real water.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She is seeing a mirage. Hot, thin air just above the tarmac bends (refracts) light from the sky upward into her eyes, so an image of the bright sky appears on the road and looks like water. There is no real water.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman near Kismaayo aims his spear below where a fish appears to be. Explain why this helps him.
realProblems.answer: Refraction bends the light from the fish as it leaves the water, so the fish appears higher and in a slightly different place than it truly is. Aiming below the apparent position corrects for the bend.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Refraction bends the light from the fish as it leaves the water, so the fish appears higher and in a slightly different place than it truly is. Aiming below the apparent position corrects for the bend.

realProblems.prompt: Halima buys a cloth that looks a rich blue by the red glow of a market lamp but looks almost black in the shop's dim red light. Explain what is happening.
realProblems.answer: An object can only reflect colours present in the light. The lamp gives mostly red light, so a blue cloth has almost no blue to reflect and looks nearly black. In true daylight, which contains blue, it would look blue again.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: An object can only reflect colours present in the light. The lamp gives mostly red light, so a blue cloth has almost no blue to reflect and looks nearly black. In true daylight, which contains blue, it would look blue again.

realProblems.prompt: Explain why wearing a white robe keeps you cooler in the strong equatorial sun than wearing a black one.
realProblems.answer: White reflects all colours and absorbs little, so it stays cool; black absorbs almost all light and turns it to heat, so a black robe gets hot.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: White reflects all colours and absorbs little, so it stays cool; black absorbs almost all light and turns it to heat, so a black robe gets hot.

realProblems.prompt: After the Gu rains the sun breaks through and a rainbow arches over the savanna. Explain, step by step, how the rainbow forms in the raindrops.
realProblems.answer: Sunlight enters each raindrop and refracts, splits into colours by dispersion, reflects off the back of the drop, and refracts again on the way out. Millions of drops each send a colour to your eyes, forming the arch. You see it with the sun behind you and rain in front.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Sunlight enters each raindrop and refracts, splits into colours by dispersion, reflects off the back of the drop, and refracts again on the way out. Millions of drops each send a colour to your eyes, forming the arch. You see it with the sun behind you and rain in front.

realProblems.prompt: A ray of light strikes a flat mirror making an angle of 65 degrees with the mirror surface. Find the angle of incidence and the angle of reflection, showing your working.
realProblems.answer: The angle is given from the surface, so the angle of incidence = 90 - 65 = 25 degrees. By the law of reflection the angle of reflection = 25 degrees (both from the normal).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The angle is given from the surface, so the angle of incidence = 90 - 65 = 25 degrees. By the law of reflection the angle of reflection = 25 degrees (both from the normal).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a luminous and a non-luminous object? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A luminous object makes its own light (e.g. the sun, a fire); a non-luminous object does not and is seen by reflected light (e.g. the moon, a goat).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the law of reflection in one sentence.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why must angles in reflection be measured from the normal and not from the mirror?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because the normal gives a fixed 90-degree reference. Measuring from the tilted mirror surface would give inconsistent, confusing angles.
Refraction is the bending of light as it changes speed when passing from one transparent material into another.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A straw in a glass of water looks bent. Explain why using the idea of refraction.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Light from the underwater part of the straw refracts (bends) as it leaves the water into the air, so the straw's lower part appears shifted, making it look bent or broken at the surface.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the two types of lens and say whether each brings light together or spreads it out.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A convex (converging) lens brings light together; a concave (diverging) lens spreads light out.

questions.question: Which of these is a luminous object?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the sun. A luminous object makes its own light; the moon, a mirror and a mango only reflect light.

questions.question: Light travels in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straight lines. This is shown by sharp shadows and narrow beams.

questions.question: The imaginary line at 90 degrees to a mirror is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: normal. The normal is drawn at 90 degrees to the surface and is the reference for all angles.

questions.question: The law of reflection says the angle of incidence equals the angle of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: reflection. The law states angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

questions.question: A ray hits a mirror at 30 degrees to the normal. The angle of reflection is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 30 degrees. By the law of reflection the reflected angle equals the incident angle.

questions.question: The bending of light as it passes from air into water is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: refraction. Refraction is the bending of light when it changes speed between materials.

questions.question: A convex lens is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: thicker in the middle. A convex (converging) lens is thicker at its centre.

questions.question: The part of the eye where the image forms is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: retina. The image forms on the light-sensitive retina at the back of the eye.

questions.question: Splitting white light into colours with a prism is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dispersion. Dispersion splits white light into its spectrum of colours.

questions.question: The three primary colours of LIGHT are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: red, green, blue. These are the primary colours of light; they add to make white.

questions.question: A red cloth looks red because it:
options: absorbs red and reflects the rest
reflects red and absorbs the rest
questions.answer: reflects red and absorbs the rest
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: reflects red and absorbs the rest. The cloth reflects only red light to your eyes.

questions.question: A blue cloth under pure red light looks:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: black. There is no blue light in a red beam for the blue cloth to reflect, so it looks black.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines and describe evidence for this, such as shadows and beams.
I can describe the difference between luminous and non-luminous objects and explain how we see non-luminous objects by reflected light.
I can state and use the law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
I can measure angles of incidence and reflection from the normal and solve simple numerical problems.
I can explain refraction as the bending of light when it changes speed passing from one material into another.
I can describe how convex and concave lenses bend light, and explain how a converging lens forms an image.
I can label the main parts of the human eye and explain how the eye focuses light onto the retina.
